title: End of module: Fields 2: Magnetic Fields and Induction

questions.text: A straight wire of length $0.40\,\text{m}$ carries a current of $5.0\,\text{A}$ perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of flux density $0.30\,\text{T}$. Calculate the force on the wire.
Using Fleming's left-hand rule for the force on a current-carrying conductor, which finger (or thumb) represents the direction of the magnetic field?
choices: None — the field has no assigned direction in the rule
questions.explanation: Fleming's left-hand rule: First finger = Field, seCond finger = Current, thuMb = Motion (force). The first finger represents the field.

questions.text: A proton ($m = 1.67 \times 10^{-27}\,\text{kg}$, $Q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$) travels at $1.0 \times 10^{7}\,\text{m s}^{-1}$ perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of flux density $0.20\,\text{T}$. Calculate the radius of its circular path.
A charged particle moves in a circular path in a region of uniform magnetic field, entering perpendicular to the field. Why does the magnetic force not change the particle's speed?
choices: The magnetic force is always perpendicular to the velocity, so it does no work and only acts as a centripetal force
The magnetic force increases the particle's speed continuously
The magnetic force acts parallel to the velocity at all times
The magnetic force is zero throughout the motion
questions.explanation: $F = BQv\sin\theta$ acts perpendicular to $v$ at every instant. A force perpendicular to velocity does no work, so it cannot change speed — it only changes direction, acting as the centripetal force for uniform circular motion.

questions.text: By Faraday's law, $\varepsilon = -N\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\Phi}{\mathrm{d}t}$. Which of the following would increase the magnitude of the induced EMF in a coil, all else being equal?
choices: Increasing the number of turns $N$ on the coil
Increasing the rate of change of flux, $\mathrm{d}\Phi/\mathrm{d}t$
Decreasing the coil's cross-sectional area while the rate of change of $B$ stays the same
In a generator, rotating the coil faster (increasing $\omega$)
questions.explanation: EMF is proportional to $N$ and to the rate of change of flux, so increasing either increases the EMF; in a generator $\varepsilon_0 = NBA\omega$, so increasing $\omega$ also increases it. Decreasing the coil's area (with $\mathrm{d}B/\mathrm{d}t$ fixed) reduces $\mathrm{d}\Phi/\mathrm{d}t = A\,\mathrm{d}B/\mathrm{d}t$, so it decreases the EMF, not increases it.

questions.text: Lenz's law states that an induced EMF always acts in a direction that opposes the change producing it. This direction is a direct consequence of which fundamental physical principle?
questions.explanation: If the induced current instead reinforced the change that caused it, the effect would keep growing and generate energy from nothing. Lenz's law — opposition to the change — is required by the **conservation of energy**.

questions.text: A flat coil of $150$ turns has cross-sectional area $0.02\,\text{m}^2$ and lies with its plane perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of flux density $0.08\,\text{T}$. Calculate the flux linkage of the coil.
questions.explanation: $\Phi = BA = 0.08 \times 0.02 = 0.0016\,\text{Wb}$ per turn. Flux linkage $= N\Phi = 150 \times 0.0016 = 0.24\,\text{Wb}$.

questions.text: The magnetic field through a single-turn coil of area $0.05\,\text{m}^2$ decreases uniformly from $0.60\,\text{T}$ to $0.20\,\text{T}$ in $0.10\,\text{s}$. Calculate the magnitude of the induced EMF.
An ideal transformer has $500$ turns on its primary coil and $100$ turns on its secondary coil. If the primary voltage is $100\,\text{V}$, calculate the secondary voltage.
An ideal step-up transformer has more turns on its secondary coil than its primary coil. Compared with the primary current, the secondary current is…
choices: Impossible to determine from this information
questions.explanation: For an ideal transformer, power is conserved: $I_pV_p = I_sV_s$. A step-up transformer has $V_s > V_p$, so to keep the product constant $I_s$ must be smaller than $I_p$.
